channel.title: Posts on mutable state
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on mutable state

item.title: On Engineering Teams and Mutable State
item.description:
The analogy I keep coming back to is this: managing an engineering team is less like writing a program and more like maintaining one that’s already running in production.

You didn’t write most of the code. Some of it is undocumented. Parts of it are brilliant. Other parts make you wince. And you can’t just stop the world and rewrite it — the system has to keep running while you improve it.

item.title: What AI Pair Programming Actually Changes
item.description:
I’ve spent several months using AI coding tools heavily — across Python, Java, and infrastructure work. Here’s what I’ve actually noticed.

The bottleneck moves

Before: I spent a lot of time writing boilerplate, looking up syntax, and translating intent into code.

After: I spend almost no time on those things. The bottleneck is now upstream — in the quality of my thinking before I touch the keyboard.

This is not a small shift. It means that knowing what to build, and why, matters more than ever. The execution gap between a senior and junior engineer used to partially mask differences in design thinking. That gap is closing fast.

item.title: The Delivery Manager Trap
item.description:
There’s a version of technical delivery management that looks productive and isn’t.

It goes like this: you run the standups, you chase the dependencies, you update the JIRA board, you write the status report, you attend the steering committee. You are busy. The team is shipping. Everything looks fine.

And then six months later you look up and realise you have no idea what the team is actually building, whether it’s the right thing, or whether any of it will matter.
